text: The surgeon begins mobilization of the liver by dividing the falciform ligament, followed by the left triangular ligament. Dissection continues to expose the roots of the main hepatic veins, revealing the main extrahepatic venous trunks. The hepatogastric ligament is opened, identifying an accessory left hepatic artery. Adhesions and the right triangular ligament are divided using diathermy, detaching the liver from the diaphragm up to the root of the right hepatic vein. An ultrasonographic study is performed to assess the tumor's extent and its relation to vascular structures, aiding in the design of the transection plane.

The surgeon disconnects the liver from the retrohepatic inferior vena cava, double tying all short hepatic veins and securing the knots with additional four-row polypropylene stitches. The liver is rotated medially by the first assistant, with the patient's central venous pressure maintained below five millimeters of mercury.

The surgeon detaches the liver from the inferior vena cava, exposing the right hepatic vein, which is encircled with a sling for isolation. The inferior vena cava is prepared for occlusion above the renal veins, and the common trunk of the left and middle hepatic vein is dissected free and encircled with a sling. All extrahepatic venous trunks are isolated, followed by the division of the cystic duct and artery, with the gallbladder remaining attached to the liver for excision with the specimen.

The surgeon prepares the hepatoduodenal ligament for occlusion and performs ischemic preconditioning of the liver with inflow occlusion and reperfusion. The portal hepatis is occluded with a Shatinsky clamp, and the accessory left hepatic artery is occluded with a Guttek clamp. Clamps are placed on the right hepatic vein and the common trunk of the left and middle hepatic veins. The transection plane is redefined with ultrasound and marked with diathermy. The liver parenchyma is divided with a scalpel, revealing a clean operating field with minimal blood loss. Vascular and biliary structures are clearly visualized and divided intrahepatically. A venous branch to the middle hepatic vein is divided, and vessel and biliary orifices on the remnant side are sutured.

The surgeon encounters and divides the portal branch to the anterior segments of the right liver lobe, followed by division of the portal branch to the posterior segments. Extreme care is taken to avoid injury to the hilar structures on the remnant side. The portal orifices are then accurately sutured with RANX-III-O polypropylene.

The surgeon divides the branch of the right hepatic vein draining the posterior segments of the right liver lobe. Sharp transection is used for partial removal of the wall of a major hepatic vein, followed by its repair. The excised specimen includes the tumor. After removal, liver outflow is restored, followed by inflow release. The cut surface is inspected for bleeding from small vessels, which is controlled with polypropylene sutures.

After achieving hemostasis, the surgeon applies fibrin glue to the cut surface of the liver and places a silastic drain nearby. The liver tissue appears to have normal consistency. The abdomen is closed in one layer using a nylon loop suture.
